steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Identify given information and angle positioning
steps.content:
We are given AB = DE = 5 cm and \angle A = \angle D = 35 degrees. In triangle ABC, angle A is located at vertex A, positioned between sides AB and AC.

In triangle DEF, angle D is located at vertex D, positioned between sides DE and DF.

To prove congruence, we need one of the standard criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL.

Since we have one side and one angle, we should consider SAS (Side-Angle-Side), which requires two sides with the angle between them.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice against congruence criteria
steps.content:
• "The lengths of sides $BC$ and $EF$ are equal." This gives us AB = DE and \angle A with side BC, which is SSA. The angle A is not between sides AB and BC, so this configuration does not guarantee congruence.
  • "The lengths of sides $AC$ and $DF$ are equal." This gives us AB = DE (side), \angle A = \angle D (angle), and AC = DF (side). Since \angle A is between sides AB and AC, and \angle D is between sides DE and DF, this is the Side-Angle-Side configuration.
  • "The measures of angles $B$ and $C$ are equal." This only provides information about angles within triangle ABC and does not allow direct comparison with triangle DEF.
  • "No additional information is necessary to prove that the two triangles are congruent." One side and one angle are not sufficient for any congruence criterion.
With AC = DF, we have SAS congruence: Side AB = DE, Angle A = D (included between the sides), Side AC = DF. That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
